speaker: interviewer
text: Date is December 7, 1995. Survivor's name is Sonia Resnick-Tetelbaum. That last name is spelled R-E-S-N-I-C-K hyphen T-E-T-E-L-B-A-U-M. Last name at birth was Walter. That's W-A-L-T-E-R. My name is Shari Maltz. We're in Toronto, Canada. Language is English. Today's December 7, 1995. Survivor's name is Sonia Resnick-Tetelbaum. Last name at birth is Walter. My name is Shari Maltz. We're in Toronto, Canada. Language is English. Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my name is Sonia Resnick-Tetelbaum. My maiden name, Walter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born? Date.

speaker: interviewee
text: The date, 9/16/23. This is my-- on the papers that I came to America. But my original date is June 30, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which makes you what today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born where? The city you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a little town-- little town. It's a village, a farmer's village, Nowosiolki Czeskie. I have all the spellings there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is near where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near, well, the town, Sienkiewiczowka where I was really went to school and all my youth was there. And the biggest, biggest city is Lutsk in Wolyn, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell me a little bit about your parents. Your, your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is-- Benja-- was Benjamin Walter. I'm getting-- and my mother's name is Malka Bella.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Krystol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Krystol was her maiden name. Did you ever know how your parents met?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really recall. It was probably through a shadchan because my father was from another town. And-- well, it wasn't far. I really don't know how they met.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they orthodox? Was it an orthodox [? town? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, see in Poland, you didn't know other way. Everybody was you know, a Jew. You were an orthodox. You didn't do anything on the Shabbat or on Yom Tov.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many kids in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my-- I had two younger brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother's name was Anchel, and my younger brother [? Froim ?] Yonah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your dad-- your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- well, like a merchant. He was buying off the farmers the wheat and selling it. And he made a living. And my mother had like a little, little department store in the house. Everything you could buy, from the needle to, to a piece of cloth. Little, you know-- they worked very, hard, very hard. But for us children, they were trying to give everything that was possible in these times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you like, your parents? Before the war, what were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they were orthodox, but they weren't-- you know, modern. We would call it here modern orthodox. And, you know, young, naturally. We had a nice, you know, family life. I mean, we had Friday night, the Shabbat, and Saturday, and holidays. And as I told you, my parents tried really very hard to give us a good edu-- good education, as much as it was possible at these times. When I was-- befo-- well my, my younger bro-- my older brother's like was 15 months just apart from me. So for about two years when I started school, I went-- my parents sent me to a little-- a town, Berestechko, where I lived with my mother's aunt and grandmother. And I went to a [GERMAN] schule, that means it's like a Hebrew [INAUDIBLE] public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malka. She was also Malka. And my grandmother, I don't remember. And there I was for about two years. And I loved it. Whatever they thought, it was in Hebrew. But then my brother got older. And my parents plain couldn't afford to send two children away. So we stayed home-- we-- I came back home. And my parents had like a-- he would call it the tutor, but it was really a teacher for Hebrew and, and Yiddish. And so they-- every season they got a teacher from the city. And there was another family, a Jewish family, in the-- that village. And they shared. A half a season the teacher stayed with us, and one half season with the others. And he-- they were very good, those teachers. The-- all the Hebrew and the Yiddish that we learned was through these teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a lot of extended family? Did you know your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my had her parents. They lived in another-- and her brother, her sister. And then another sister-- otherwise, two sisters. One of the sisters lived in the town of Druzkopol. And my father had-- was from-- was born-- I mean, raised in Druzkopol. And so he had his family there. He had sis--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did everybody gather for holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- usually in my grandparents' house we gathered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your grandparents names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my grandmother was To-- Toba, Tova was probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Krystol. But she was a-- her maiden name, I remember, was [? Furst. ?] And my grandfather was Yaakov [INAUDIBLE] Yaakov Krystol.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other side, you don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they died. I was already named after my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was Shabbaat like in your house, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. We lived in that little village--

speaker: interviewer
text: In a house? It was a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a little house in that farms village. And when it came Shabbat, so the-- from the neighborhood, from other little villages, the Jews, Jewish men came together in one house. And they had like a minyanim. And they prayed. And after-- and we children actually came with them. Then we came home and was a meal with zemirot, with everything, just like in the orthodox families is then. And life was going-- you know, for us children it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do? Did you-- what were some of you hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we were f-- the day was full as children. We went to public school in the morning. And in the afternoon, we had the Hebrew. Now, on the Shabbat, like, we used to come together-- children, we got older, like 12, 11, 12, 13. And you know, like children, play all kind of games. And then my grandparents lived in the Sienkiewiczowka. There were mother-- mother had-- her brother and her sister, they also had a family. So most of the time when I would get older I used to spend my Shabbat and Yom Tov and vacations with my grandparents, and because I didn't want to be in the farm village. It didn't, you know, didn't have no Jewish children to play. It was different. So I used to be mostly there. And sometimes, vacation time, I also went to Druzkopol. That's the other part of the family lived. And until 1939, everything was going smoothly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do when you were growing up? What did you do in the summers?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm trying to tell you. In the summers, I went most of the time to Druzkopol and stayed with my mother's sister. And she had a family, and I had friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in your town that you grew up in, was it mostly Jew-- it was mostly non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish. We were only two families in that village. But in, in nearby, this town of Sienkiewiczowka, where my mother had the family, there where I had friends. Then, you know, friends-- there were a lot more Jews. The whole town had maybe 400 to 500 people, Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were growing up, did you-- were you involved in any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. See it was, you know, like a-- when the-- in 1939 when the war broke out between Poland and Germany, I was about 14, going on my 15th. And then the Russians came in. You know, they divided Poland. Part of the Poland where we lived, the Russian occupied.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the Russian occupied, for us children it was-- like for me especially, we, we finished public school. My parents couldn't afford to send us both to high school. So it meant that my brother would have gone to high school. First of all, he was a man, a boy. And he was a very bright student, very smart boy. And I didn't have already what to do. But when the Russian came in, we went back to school, to high school. And for me, the two years when the Russian occupation was-- from '39 to '41-- that was my best years of my youth, because I went back to school. I had a lot of friends. I stayed-- we stayed, my brother and I, in Sienkiewiczowka. And as I told you, these are my best years that I remember as a growing up girl. And then-- shall I continue? No, well, yeah. But we'll-- just going back for one second, before that happened, before the war broke out and the Russians came in. Do you remember growing up, was there ever any instances of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the, the villa-- the farm village where I grew up were Czechish people, like there's Ukrainian, Polish, Czech. This was a Czechish farm, farm village. They were very well off. They were good workers. And they had very good farm. They were rich, to the Polish standard. And they did not show any anti-Semite. I do not remember any.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does Czechish mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech, like from Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they were Czech.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was colonies like this in Poland. And thanks to these Czechs, the few Jews that sur-- because from Wolyn, where I come from, very few Jews survived. And thanks to the Czechish people, we, we, we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you think made them different?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nice people. They're as-- there is-- they are different than Poles or Ukrainian. They didn't carry any, you know, against Jews. Fall-- later in the story, when I'll tell you what they did for us, it's remarkable. They were very good people. I don't know if a Jew would do for them what they did for us. That's to put it plainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were growing up, you had non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had non-Jewish friends, sure. I had very-- I was very friendly with the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents, as well, they had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did business, and they had very good friends, the Czechs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when things started to get bad, and when you start to hear about the rise of Hitler, do remember anybody talking about it in your house? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Oh sure. A lot of it was talking about it. But you know, it's like this. It's far away. You don't think it's going to happen in your house, or in your-- in your city. And you listened. And you knew. But nothing was done about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- when you were going to school, what, what did you want to be later on in life?

speaker: interviewee
text: A teacher. I used to think a teacher or a stewardess. That's, I remember, was my dreams.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so going back to what was happening in your house, do you-- did you remember if your father had or your parents had any plans to do anything? Did they ever think about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. It was talk-- there was always talking that the younger generation-- younger people wanted to go to Israel. There were people going to Ha Sharas already that I remember. But it was older, the older people than I. And everybody wanted one day-- was hoping to be in Israel one day. But there is no-- was no way of-- there were a few people that left that I know. But not my parents. Didn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- do you remember the feelings you felt as a young person about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean before the German occupation? I really-- it was-- I really didn't-- I couldn't think that I had any opinion about it. I just listened. I wanted-- I was dreaming. I would like to go to Israel. But not really that to do something about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1939, what month was it that the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: When did they come? In, in June. Can I look it up? I think it's-- 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: June?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in June 22 or July '22. July was probably-- no June '22 because it was just before, before the war-- before school, because we went back to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so in June 1922, the Russians occupied your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of Poland, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when they came in? Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, saw the soldiers caming in-- coming in. If-- well, it was better than the other side, the German side, naturally. So we felt better. But you see, the Russian also gave problems. We, children, didn't feel it. But my parents, were-- had-- you know, they were merchants. And they don't believe in it. That they right away made them, you know-- that they are-- they think that they were very rich. And a lot of-- when, when you were rich, by [? policy, ?] they'll send away a lot of Jews to Russia. Thanks to them, these people survived-- didn't, didn't fall into German. And so but since we were in this village, this-- the, the farm village, the Czechs were very nice. And I don't know if father bought a cow. And, and they said that he's also a farmer. You know, make believe that he's a-- we didn't have land, just a little garden-like. But started working like on this garden. And he wasn't anymore a merchant. And so my parents weren't that happy about that. But it was better than--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he stop being a merchant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Germ-- Russian don't believe in merchants at that time. Everything was by them-- like there was no stores, groceries. They had their, their own cooperatives. And they had people working for them. And that's where you got something. It was very scary, everything together-- nothing, they didn't bring in merchandise. They liquidated merchandise from the merchants, you know? And then they sold it. Or they took it-- they took it for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did your father do for a living at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I told you. He got a cow. And he got a little land. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Became a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Became like a farmer, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me before that so '39 to '41 was your best school year.

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: For children. Because we went-- I didn't-- if, if by normal time, by Pole, I didn't have what to do. I couldn't go back to school. So I would have been home. And I don't know what I-- there was no future for me at all. Here, I went back to school. And I had friends. And I had a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from '39 to '41, while you were back in school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hearing-- did anyone come to town from other parts of Poland to tell you what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was very few-- there were some that ran away from Poland. And they told us that it's-- what's going on. But not on a such a big-- we didn't know much. And these that came from Poland, the Russians send them away to Russia. Or some of them went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, did you, yourself, ever know and of the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I knew my teachers were Russian, from Russia. And, and then we had-- we went to the same school with others. There were Russian children in the school too. So we were friends just like everybody with other children. Didn't make no difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from '39 to '41, while under Russian occupation, were you still allowed to practice your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not legal. Oh, well, it wasn't like-- let's say, my brother had to be bar mitzvah. So my father took him to shul, like on a Thursday morning. He didn't make no-- anything out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were still synogogues. People still get it-- get it for Saturdays, Sundays. But the young people, they they tried to talk them out of it to become communists. They had youth, youth organizations, you know? So from '39 to '41, did you still see your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I lived with my grandparents actually, because I went to Sienkiewiczowka. And we went there to school, my brother and I. And my parents still remained in Nowosiolki Czeskie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for your extended family, life was continuing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my grandparents and my aunt and uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other restrictions put on you during that time period?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941, When the war broke out between Russia and Germany, that's really the whole problem started. That was of September of '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in September '41 to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, well, I was-- we, we were in Sienkiewiczowka. But then we had to come back to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to Nowosiolki Czeskie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this? Anchel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anchel, yeah. And then we came back, we wanted-- a lot of young people escaped to Russia with-- with the Russian army. And a lot people went away. We wanted to go. We wanted to m-- the family should go, the parents. But my parents didn't want to. And they didn't let us go. We were young, 17 and 15. And they didn't let us. So we remained. And that's what started. It didn't take-- in a few weeks, they were told all the Jews from the farm villages to go to the city. They sent us to the city of Druzkopol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German. They had already there-- the Ukrainian were their helpers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so at what point did the Russians leave and the Germans come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: September.

speaker: interviewer
text: September '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And I think in October, maybe in a-- in a few weeks, they told us to go into ghe-- to the ghetto. I mean, they didn't call it the ghetto. They told us to leave. So we, we had to pack up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day that the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: September-- can I look at it? If I have the date. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] '41. 22Nd of June, that was the day. Seems like the-- all right then, I mixed it up. It's 22nd of June in 1941, the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what that day was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was horrible for us. But we were between the Czechs. I remember staying, staying with all the group of people. And just waiting as the German coming in on motorcycles. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like.

speaker: interviewee
text: They looked healthy, you know, nice dress, motorcycles, and their uniforms. And at that-- at that point, they didn't know that I'm Jewish or I'm not Jewish. I stand with all the Christian, the Czechish people. In fact, I remember somebody asked about the Jews. And he didn't answer anything to the German. But it started bad. We were afraid to sleep in the house. Used to go away from the house for the night because you heard already all kind of stories. And then in S-- in October, they send us to-- they told us to leave the, the farm village, the house, our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how, how long did you have to get out of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in days. I don't remember exactly. It was probably two, three days. They didn't give us much. And these farmers gave us forward and loaded us-- we had a horse and a carriage. And they loaded us up with food. And we went. We went to Druzkopol.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother had a sister. So we, we stayed with them in an apartment. She had her own four children. They had-- it was probably like three rooms-- three rooms. So we were there, a family, and their family. And there was another family. This was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Druzkopol. That's where we were in ghetto. A few streets they blocked up. And all the Jews have to move in in those streets. And they were taking the men and even women and girls to all kind of work, like on the roads, cleaning the offices, you know, all dirty work. And they didn't pay. We didn't have no food. It was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went to work too, sure. But he was lucky. They did not send him away. In fact, while we were living there, there was-- from October and in December, just before Hanukkah time-- somebody came into the house. You know, one of the children said, there were German taking men away. So my mother was scared. She wanted to see. So she ran out of the house-- it was winter-- didn't get dressed and went out to see if, if it's true. And my father was hiding. He was home then. And my brother, she told him to hide. And she went out. Till she came back-- it was only a false alarm. But you get very, very sick. That was on a Thursday. And she lost-- she had very high temperature. And she couldn't speak. She lost-- she was-- I think must've been like a stroke because she couldn't-- she didn't speak. And it took two days. Saturday she died. There was no doctor in the village, in that Druzkopol.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to get a permit to go to a bigger cit-- city. Until we got a permit that we can take her, she died. Maybe better, so she didn't have to go through all that, that we went through later when people, you know. Better to die in your own bed than to be killed by the Germans. So you-- she's buried in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Druzkopol. I was-- I never saw my mother's grave. The, the, the day of the burial-- it must have been Sunday-- I got very bad pain, stomach pains. At that time I didn't know what it is. But such a pain, that I couldn't even be at the levaya. But later life, I knew what it was. It was gall-- my first gall bladder attack. By 17, I had a gall bladder attack. Then, we were there by my-- with our family. But then my mother had a brother in Sienkiewiczowka. Sienkiewiczowka was much easier. These-- Druzkopol was surrounded with Ukrainian. Ukrainian, this-- they were the worst. They were worse than the Germans because they knew every-- whoever is a Jew. And any little thing, they killed right away, and slaughtered, and took away everything. You know, every time they came out, with-- you give them gold. You give them the furs. And, you know, you couldn't-- a Jew couldn't have anything. Had to give them all away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my, my uncle-- my mother's brother that lived in Sienkiewiczowka-- got a permit for us to come to live with them, because he knew we are near the Czechish people, would be easier for us. So middle of the night, one night, my father, my two brothers, and myself left Druzkopol. And we went-- I don't know-- it must have took about 7, 8 hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a Ukrainian that we paid. My father was probably had some kind of money. And he took us by horse and carriage to Sienkiewiczowka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you under a tarp or something? Or were you hiding? Or you're just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, we didn't-- it was middle of the night. We were on the wagon. And we left the town. The day after we left the town, this ghetto, Druzkopol, was wired. And no-- and there was no one survivor after from Druzkopol.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many-- how many Jews were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this ghetto, I think it must have been about 1,000 Jews. And nobody survived from that ghetto. They wired about this so terribly. And it didn't take long till they killed all the Jews. It was in like in December-- no, we left, I think it's in Mar-- in January, March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how your father and you-- how did you all react to your mother's death at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it was a very-- you know, I got sick. And my father had to be the strong one. He had two younger children. And there was no-- see-- well, let me go a little bit back. While we were in Druzkopol in ghetto, my mother used to take-- we wore yellow stars. My mother used to take off the yellow stars from the front and get dressed like a-- you know, like a farmer and go to the village, the Czechish village and get some food. And the good Czechs did give her food. Not only did she got food for us, she took some food and took it to her fa-- father and mother. They were old people. Some-- and from there, she came home. I don't know how she did it. Because it was a long way. But she brought us some food. That was before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- where am I? [LAUGHS] And then we came back to Sienkiewiczowka, my father had to back to work. My brother and I also went, but not that much, because there was no mother in the house, so they-- little-- I had to clean and, you know, do everything. And we're talking about what date about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '42 already, in this-- like in February of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what we'll do? We'll pick up on what happened after. We'll just take a quick break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK.
